In the argument of Wallerstein (1980), absolutism was invented precisely when absolute power had become a historical impossibility. Neither of these criticisms, however important, disqualify what we try to argue here. That we cannot construct ourselves from cosmic exile, and that states, then as now, remained context-dependent systems of power is so obviously true that we have to wonder why the idea of self-enclosed selves and states was ever invented in the first place. This question, however, can hardly be answered by examining any real ‘substance’ of self and state, or by demonstrating externals such as nature, culture, un-reason, globalization, or any other type of polluting otherness. To answer the question we can instead look to the succession of forms that the concepts of state and self have taken, consider the conditions of their emergence, and their long term effects. The idea of self-autonomy may be a fiction, but it remains a highly powerful one. The Shared Conditions of Emergence: Early Modernity as Liminal Period In other words, if the logic of the absolute state and self stands in contradiction to practice and common-sense, and possibly even to the logics of its own argument (the ‘Cartesian circle’ corresponds closely to the Hobbesian contract made by men already willing to make the contract; they are a view from nowhere), we must place it against the problem it was meant to solve. The problem that had become unbearable during the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries has already been outlined above. It was a religious crisis, originating in the waning of the Middle Ages, and it touched all the basic relations between God and man, society and nature. Protestantism, puritan and revivalist as it was, it became one answer to the dissolution of order. Following the religious schisms, Europe entered a century and half of continuous warfare. This warfare took on international dimensions, but led also to ceaseless civil wars within the European polities, as in France and England, the birthplaces of Descartes and Hobbes. The Hobbesian problem of order was therefore not some distant abstract, philosophical preoccupation, neither was the general shattering of truth that worried Descartes. Both Hobbes and Descartes developed their thoughts during the Thirty Years’ War. The continued existence of war and unrest was their ‘normal’ background experience. Historians will continue to discuss whether these wars were really religious in character or whether struggles over religious doctrine mostly served as a pretext for the reorganization of power. From the Liminality and the Modern 134 perspective provided here, the important fact to stress is that the wars and the social and political revolutions of the seventeenth century were typically liminal in character. Arguments over religious doctrine may therefore have served as cover for wider power struggles, without altering the socio-religious character of the larger crisis and the answers given to it. Truths about the world which were so obviously culturally and historically determined were elevated to absolute truths and employed by all involved parties to claim their just cause, and to draw the sword in the name of that justice. Without any ordered centre of ‘truth-power’, this slippery use of truth and reason had become mimetically dangerous. It had to stop. Some kind of first principle for truth-measurement and power-use was required. The search for a principle of equality, for an overall figure or notion of judgment with an undifferentiated measure of justice and truth was equally fundamental to Descartes and Hobbes. It was a quest of their age. The novelty of the warfare that plagued Europe from the sixteenth century was not only its temporal duration, but also its increasing involvement of the human masses. Hobbes was fearful of the civil wars that managed to drag the masses into strife by tying the personally felt truth of the believer to the re-organization of power. This emotional involvement of the masses, and the uncontrollable situations it led to, even within singular polities, represented something new and frightening in European history. If, as proposed here, such an age can be seen as a period of liminality, the crystallization of ideas that took place during this period must be given special attention. Following van Gennep’s tripartite structure, once the liminal period is closed, the ideas and practices that have been established will settle as new form and take on the quality of structure. History in such a view is not a continuous stream of action governed by a structure that changes only slowly, if at all. Rather, there are historical moments in which structure is loose, and there are other moments in which structure takes on the quality of doxa, where it becomes frozen. Drawing on the anthropological understanding of ritual liminality, the playfulness of the period of liminality is at one and the same time unstructured and highly structuring: the most basic rules of behaviour are questioned, doubt and scepticism as to the existence of the world are radicalized, but the problematizations, the formative experiences and the reformulations of being during the period of liminality proper will feed the individual (and his or her cohort) with a new structure and set of rules that, once established, will glide back to the level of the taken-for-granted. This follows Weber’s approach to the analysis of ideas and ‘images’, closely linked to their rationalization and systematization by intellectuals. Voegelin’s analysis of the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries is in fact quite in line with this interpretation, and so are his conceptual terms. He calls the period from the early sixteenth century to the middle of the seventeenth century ‘The Great Confusion’, the subtitles given to Volumes IV (Renaissance and Reformation) and V (Religion and Modernity) in his History of Political Ideas manuscripts.
